content.problemStatement:
Chef has two numbers AAA and BBB.

In one operation, Chef can choose either AAA or BBB and multiply it by 222.

Determine whether he can make both AAA and BBB equal after any number (possibly, zero) of moves.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two space-separated integers AAA and BBB.


Output Format
For each test case, output `YES` if Chef can make both numbers equal, `NO` otherwise.


Note that the checker is case-insensitive i.e. `YES`, `Yes`, `yes`, `yES` are all considered same.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers A and B.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if Chef can make A and B equal by multiplying either number by 2 any number of times, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: 5's odd part is 5, 20 -> 5 after removing 2s, so YES.
Test case 2: numbers are already equal.
Test case 3: odd parts are 3 and 1, different → NO.
Test case 4: odd parts are 25 and 5, different → NO.
1 and 1 are equal → YES.\n3 (odd 3) and 6 (odd 3) → YES.\n7 (odd 7) and 10 (odd 5) → NO.
40 -> odd part 5, matches 5 → YES.\n45 and 90 both have odd part 45 → YES.

solution.approach: Multiplying a number by 2 only adds factors of 2 to its prime factorisation; the odd part (the number after removing all factors of 2) never changes. Therefore A can be turned into any number of the form A * 2^k and B into B * 2^l. The two numbers can become equal iff their odd parts are identical. Algorithm: for each test case repeatedly divide A by 2 while it is even, do the same for B, then compare the resulting values. If equal print YES, else NO.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * log(max(A,B))) – each division by 2 reduces the number, at most 30‑31 steps for 10^9.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
